---
title: "What should I study to get better at Jeopardy?"
description: "Scraping 40 years of J-Archive, then clustering 123k categories to find what to learn."
date: "2026-07-06"
categories: [data science, nlp, jeopardy]
image: thumbnail.png
draft: true
---

I like Jeopardy, and I wanted a data-driven answer to a simple question: of everything the
show could ask me, *what is actually worth studying?* This post walks through how I scraped
40 years of the show, turned every category into something a computer can compare, grouped
the categories into "types," named them, and used the result to find where studying pays off.

Every figure below is generated from a committed dataset — no live scraping — so the whole
post re-renders from scratch.

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import plotly.express as px

clues = pd.read_parquet("clues.parquet")
clusters = pd.read_parquet("category_clusters.parquet")
summary = pd.read_parquet("cluster_summary.parquet")
labels = pd.read_csv("cluster_labels.csv").set_index("cluster_id")["name"].to_dict()
tokens = pd.read_parquet("category_tokens.parquet")

plt.rcParams["figure.dpi"] = 110
```

## Scraping J-Archive

There's no API, so the data comes from a polite, cached crawl of
[J-Archive](https://j-archive.com): a `fetch` stage (one request/second, every page cached
to disk), a `parse` stage that reads each game's board out of the HTML, and a resumable
`crawl` that walks every season and game. The raw crawl stays on my machine; what ships is
a single compact `clues.parquet` — one row per clue — so anyone (including the CI that
builds this site) starts from identical data without re-scraping.

The result is **563,266 clues** across **9,485 games**, spanning 1984 to 2026.

```{python}
#| label: fig-overview
#| fig-cap: "The corpus: clues per year, game types, and rounds."
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(13, 4))

per_year = clues["air_date"].dt.year.value_counts().sort_index()
axes[0].bar(per_year.index, per_year.values, color="#4C72B0")
axes[0].set_title("Clues per year")

gt = clues["game_type"].value_counts().head(6)[::-1]
axes[1].barh(gt.index, gt.values, color="#55A868")
axes[1].set_title("Clues by game type (top 6)")

rounds = clues["round"].value_counts()
axes[2].bar(rounds.index, rounds.values, color="#C44E52")
axes[2].set_title("Clues by round")
axes[2].tick_params(axis="x", rotation=20)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```
